text: We present a video of a laparoscopic right colectomy following an ilio-orthotopic bladder substitute constructed close to twenty years ago.

The challenges associated with performing a right colectomy after a Studer pouch include lysing adhesions, keeping the vascular supply of the pouch intact, avoiding injury to the pouch during dissection, and achieving an appropriate oncologic resection.
There is a lack of published literature on these challenges.
To perform a studer pouch, you would isolate an ileo segment, fifty-four to sixty centimeters long, approximately twenty-five centimeters proximal to the ileocecal valve.
The surgeon performs an ileo-ileo anastomosis to restore bowel continuity, connecting two segments of the ileum using sutures or staples.
The surgeon splits the ureters and performs an anastomosis to the proximal unopened part of the isolated ileo segment.
The surgeon creates an opening in the caudal part of the reservoir, preparing it for anastomosis to the urethra.
The patient is a seventy-seven-year-old male who underwent a diagnostic colonoscopy and was noted to have a large polyp in the right colon over the ileocecal valve extending into the terminal ileum.
The patient's past history was significant for ilio-orthotopic bladder, also called a suitor pouch, constructed nearly seventeen years ago for bladder cancer.
The biopsy of the polyp returned as tubulovilous adenoma and this was deemed to be endoscopically unresectable.
He was referred for surgical intervention and a CT of the abdomen pelvis was obtained for operative planning.
The CT scan showed thickening over the ileocecal valve consistent with the presence of the polyp.
The video shows the ileoanastomosis and the segment of the ileum used for neobladder reconstruction, highlighting the anatomical structures involved in the surgical field.
The patient was counseled regarding laparoscopic right colectomy with possibility of conversion to open.
Urological consultation regarding placement of bilateral ureteral catheters through the neobladder was sought and deemed to be impossible.
The urologist felt that placing stents into reimplanted ureters would be too difficult to find, cannulate, and pass stents into as they could easily coil.
Also, neocystoscopy is challenging due to the altered anatomy.

A VisiPort is placed in the left upper quadrant, and adhesions between the omentum and abdominal wall are dissected, revealing multiple loops of small bowel adhered to the abdominal wall, particularly in the right lower quadrant.
A 12mm port is placed superumbilically through a small incisional hernia defect, providing access for the laparoscopic procedure.
The video shows the ilio-ilio anastomosis, highlighted by the presence of anastomotic staples securing the connection between the ileal segments.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to carefully separate adhesions between the small bowel loops and the abdominal wall, avoiding the use of energy devices near the small bowel.
The surgeon identifies the Studer pouch by instilling 400 milliliters of saline diluted with methylene blue through a Foley catheter, which remains clamped during the procedure.
The methylene blue solution is visibly staining the wall of the Studer pouch, indicating its presence within the pouch.
The surgical view displays the ileocolic junction and the ileo-ileo anastomosis, located approximately twelve centimeters from the ileocecal valve, with visible anastomotic staples.
The surgeon places a 5mm port in the right mid-abdominal region to facilitate dissection and retraction during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects posterior to the ileoileal anastomosis, carefully separating the loops of small bowel to expose the posterior wall of the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection between the loops of small bowel and the antireflux nipple valve, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon begins lateral to medial mobilization of the colon due to obscured visibility of the iliocolic pedicle caused by adhesions.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the colon along the white line of Toldt, progressing towards the hepatocolic ligament.
The surgeon separates the omentum from the transverse colon and continues mobilizing the colon until reaching the duodenum.
The surgeon continues the dissection medially, revealing the right ureter adjacent to the antireflux nipple valve.

The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery to isolate the ileocolic pedicle, ensuring the dissection is distant from the duodenum. The vasculature is confirmed to supply only the terminal ileum and colon.
The surgeon uses a harmonic energy device to transect the ileocolic pedicle, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon assesses the perfusion of the Studer pouch after completing previous mobilization steps, ensuring adequate blood flow to the structure.
The surgeon injects indocyanine green to confirm the perfusion of the studer pouch and nipple valve, ensuring they remain intact after the ileocolic pedicle is taken down.
The surgeon divides the remaining ileo and colonic mesentery using a harmonic scalpel, completing the separation of these structures.

The surgeon externalizes the specimen, including the ileoileal anastomosis and six centimeters of ileum proximal to it. An extracorporeal anastomosis is constructed, and indocyanine green is injected to confirm that the anastomosis is well perfused.
The surgeon returns the externalized specimen inside the abdominal cavity and confirms hemostasis, ensuring no active bleeding is visible.
The surgical field shows a hemostatic pedicle and an intact duodenum. Indocyanine green is used to confirm that the Studer pouch and antireflux nipple valve are well vascularized.
No spillage of urine was noted during the operation.
The patient made an uneventful recovery and was discharged on post-operative day two.
He required replacement of the Foley catheter on post-op day one for urinary retention, which was later removed on post-op day seven.
Final pathology showed a twelve centimeter tubulovilous adenoma over the ileocecal valve extending all the way up to the ilioilial anastomosis.
The patient was last seen in the colorectal clinic two weeks postoperatively.
He was progressing appropriately without any late ischemic injuries to the neobladder noted.
In summary, our video demonstrates a laparoscopic right colectomy following an ilio-orthotopic neobladder.
